coredata.dc:title: Determination of methomyl in grain using deep eutectic solvent-based extraction combined with fluorescence-based enzyme inhibition assays
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yan
Wang, Huihui
Chen, Zhenjia
Jing, Xu
Wang, Xiaowen
coredata.dc:description: A deep eutectic solvent (DES)-based extraction method is established to facilitate the determination of methomyl in grain via enzyme inhibition fluorescence. The environmentally-friendly DES was synthesized from proline and ethylene glycol and used as a green replacement for traditional extraction solvents that are generally toxic. The DES was added to grain samples and vortex extraction of methomyl, the supernatant was then collected for fluorescence detection. Biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized from millet were used as fluorescent probes. Acetylcholinesterase catalyzes the hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine iodide to thiocholine. The positively-charged thiocholine interacts electrostatically with the negatively-charged quantum dots resulting in the quenching of their fluorescent emission. The pesticide extract solution blocks the enzyme activity and thus recovers the fluorescent from the quantum dots. The fluorescence response was correlated with the amount of methomyl residue in the grain over the range 0.01 to 5 mg kg−1. The limit of detection was found to be 0.003 mg kg−1, and the limit of quantification 0.01 mg kg−1. Recoveries of 86.5% to 107.8% were obtained using real samples, including millet, rice, wheat, and barley, with a relative standard deviation of less than 3.8%. The method is efficient and convenient and has good application prospects for extracting and detecting pesticides in grain samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Methomyl
Grain
Deep eutectic solvent
Biomass carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of biomass used to generate CQDs
Effect of the pH of the phosphate buffer
Effect of the interaction time between the TC and biomass CQDs
Effect of incubation time between the methomyl solution and AChE
Analysis of methomyl in different grains

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Intensity of the fluorescence produced: (a) without, and (b) with the addition of methomyl.
Determination of methomyl in different grain samples.

head.ce:title: Determination of methomyl in grain using deep eutectic solvent-based extraction combined with fluorescence-based enzyme inhibition assays

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shanxi Agricultural University, Taigu, Shanxi 030801, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shanxi Agricultural University, Taigu, Shanxi 030801, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shanxi Agricultural University, Shanxi 030801, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A deep eutectic solvent (DES)-based extraction method is established to facilitate the determination of methomyl in grain via enzyme inhibition fluorescence. The environmentally-friendly DES was synthesized from proline and ethylene glycol and used as a green replacement for traditional extraction solvents that are generally toxic. The DES was added to grain samples and vortex extraction of methomyl, the supernatant was then collected for fluorescence detection. Biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized from millet were used as fluorescent probes. Acetylcholinesterase catalyzes the hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine iodide to thiocholine. The positively-charged thiocholine interacts electrostatically with the negatively-charged quantum dots resulting in the quenching of their fluorescent emission. The pesticide extract solution blocks the enzyme activity and thus recovers the fluorescent from the quantum dots. The fluorescence response was correlated with the amount of methomyl residue in the grain over the range 0.01 to 5 mg kg−1. The limit of detection was found to be 0.003 mg kg−1, and the limit of quantification 0.01 mg kg−1. Recoveries of 86.5% to 107.8% were obtained using real samples, including millet, rice, wheat, and barley, with a relative standard deviation of less than 3.8%. The method is efficient and convenient and has good application prospects for extracting and detecting pesticides in grain samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Methomyl is a carbamate insecticide that is widely-used to treat farmland crops. It has broad biological activity and is highly effective against insects [1]. Methomyl is highly soluble in water and therefore has a high contamination potential in the ecosystem. The World Health Organization and European Union have classified methomyl as a toxic and hazardous pesticide [2].

Methomyl is an inhibitor of the enzyme acetylcholinesterase (AChE) [3]. It thus reduces the ability of the enzyme to hydrolyze acetylcholine, leading to an accumulation of acetylcholine in the body. The main symptoms caused by methomyl are tearing of the eyes, nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain, loss of consciousness (coma), and finally, death due to respiratory failure [4,5]. Methomyl also disrupts the endocrine system due to its ability to affect estrogen production and hence reproductive ability [6].

Currently, the main methods used to determine methomyl concentration include gas chromatography–mass spectrometry [7], ultrahigh–performance liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry [8] and use of electrochemical sensors [9], etc. These methods are highly sensitive and precise. However, they are also expensive and time-consuming to use and require complex operations. Therefore, the establishment of an efficient and convenient method for determining methomyl is of primary interest.

The most time–consuming step in the analysis process is sample pretreatment. Pretreatment, however, is vital as a suitable method of sample pretreatment benefits both the qualitative and quantitative analysis of the target analytes in a complex matrix [10–12]. Liquid–liquid extraction (LLE) [13] is an emerging method of sample pretreatment that, unfortunately, uses toxic organic liquids as extraction solvents. Such solvents can be harmful to both the environment and human health. In contrast, deep eutectic solvents (DESs) can be readily recovered, are biodegradable, and can hence be regarded as green alternatives to toxic extraction solvents [14,15]. Deep eutectic solvents have the same excellent physicochemical properties as ionic liquids, but better overcome the disadvantages of complex, energy-consuming, and costly synthesis of ionic liquids. They are synthesized by combining a hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA), e.g. a quaternary ammonium salt with a hydrogen bond donor (HBD), e.g. an amide, carboxylic acid, or polyol, in appropriate stoichiometric ratios. DESs have melting points that are significantly lower than the melting points of each individual component. They are proving to have great application potential in green extraction technology.

Enzyme-based sensors are simple, fast, and convenient to use. Cholinesterase is used as a recognition element mainly for the assay of their inhibitors [16]. In this context, it is noteworthy that carbamate pesticides can specifically inhibit the activity of cholinesterase [17]. That is, residues of carbamate pesticides in grain samples hinder the activity of cholinesterase and this can be used to determine their concentration [18]. In such enzymatic assays, a substrate and chromogen are mixed to produce a color-change (which is observed and quantified) or some other physicochemical signal is used to determine the concentration of a particular compound. Either way, the amount of carbamate pesticide residue present can be subsequently determined. Because cholinesterase is storage unstable and easily inactivated at uncomfortable conditions, care should be taken to control the operating conditions during the enzyme-based assay.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are nanomaterials that compared to traditional quantum dots, do not contain toxic metal ions. They also have good fluorescence stability and fluoresce stably when continuously irradiated over a long period of time [19]. Their excellent biocompatibility and chemical durability compared with semiconductor quantum dots make them suitable for use as analytical sensors [20]. Furthermore, biomass materials are cheap and readily-available renewable resources that are rich in carbon. They are therefore good materials for preparing a variety of different CQDs. Thus, energy-saving and environmentally-friendly biomass CQDs has great application potential in food analysis.

Quantum dot sensing is based on the physical or chemical reaction between the quantum dots and the target analyte leads to enhanced or quenched luminescence [21]. Enzyme biosensors convert the concentration of an analyte into a digital signal through a specific physical/chemical transducer and signal amplification device [22]. The combined use of CQDs and enzymes can therefore serve as a prominent fluorescent probe for quantifying various pesticides in the field of food safety. Furthermore, they have high sensitivity and short response times, and they are also relatively inexpensive.

In this paper, a DES-based method for extracting methomyl from various grains is developed. An environmentally-friendly DES synthesized from L-proline and ethylene glycol is used as the extraction solvent to replace traditional organic solvents such as methanol and acetonitrile. The methomyl extracted is determined via enzymatic inhibition fluorescence. Biomass CQDs (synthesized using the one-step hydrothermal method) are used as fluorescent probes, providing a new direction for the development of biomass materials. In the experimental process, appropriate extraction solvent and CQD types should be screened, in order to achieve efficient extraction and detection of methomyl in grains.

The general principles underlying the method are illustrated in Fig. 1 . AChE catalyzes the hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine iodide (ATC) to thiocholine (TC). The positively-charged TC then interacts electrostatically with the negatively-charged biomass CQDs which quenches the emission of fluorescence from the biomass CQDs. Methomyl inhibits this enzyme activity which prevents the generation of TC and restores the fluorescence from the biomass CQDs.
In this paper, the extraction and determination methods were verified, optimized, and combined to quantitatively detect methomyl in a variety of grains.

Methomyl (99.9%) and (electric eel) AChE were supplied by Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). L-proline, ethylene glycol, oxalic acid, ethylenediamine, glycolic acid, aminoacetic acid, ethanolamine ATC, disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, and methanol were supplied by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

A multifunctional microplate reader (SpectraMax i3x, Molecular Devices Ltd., Silicon Valley, USA) was used to measure the fluorescence A Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Tensor 27 Bruker Instruments, Ltd., Beijing, China) was used to record quantum dots IR spectra. A Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 system (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Malvern, UK) was used to determine the zeta potential of the quantum dots. The IKA Votex2 vortex mixer was purchased from IKA Instruments, Ltd. (Guangzhou, China).

The experimental error was estimated by measurement uncertainty and repeatability uncertainty [23]. Measurement uncertainty is the systematic uncertainty of an instrument, that is, the error in the average reading of the instrument. Repeatability uncertainty is the variation in a measured variable represented by two standard deviations [24] . These errors were combined by the square root to represent 95% confidence intervals. The uncertainties of the measuring instruments are shown in Table S1.

L-proline (as HBA) was mixed with a variety of HBDs (ethylene glycol, oxalic acid, ethylenediamine, glycolic acid, aminoacetic acid, and ethanolamine) in different molar ratios. The mixtures were heated at 85 °C until they became transparent. They were then cooled and stored at room temperature.

Millet, rice, wheat, and corn were selected as carbon sources to synthesize biomass CQDs. A sample of the grain (2 g) was first pulverized, added to distilled water (30 mL). The sample was then heated at 200 °C in a 50-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for 6 h. After filtration through a 0.22-μm filtration membrane, the biomass CQD solution obtained was diluted 100-fold and stored at 4 °C for further use.

A grain sample (0.1 g) and a portion of the selected DES (150 µL) were added to a 1.5-mL centrifuge tube and vortexed for 90 s to thoroughly mix the two together. The supernatant formed after centrifuging the mixture (3,260g for 2 min) was collected and used in subsequent analysis experiments.

A 50 µL portion of the supernatant extracted above was incubated with 25 µL of AChE (50 mU mL−1) in a 96-well plate at 37 °C for 30 min. Then, 75 µL of a phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.8, 0.2 mmoL L–1), 50 µL of ATC (1 mmoL L–1), and 50 µL of biomass CQD solution were added and the mixture incubated for a further 15 min at 37 °C. The fluorescence emitted by the sample was then assayed using excitation/emission wavelengths of 315/370 nm.

The efficiency with which a target analyte is extracted is influenced by the physicochemical properties of the DES (diffusivity, solubility, viscosity, surface tension, polarity, etc.) [25]. Therefore, it is crucial to select an appropriate DES to use as extraction solvent. To this end, six different types of DES were designed (Table S2).

It was found that L-proline (Pro) can form DESs with ethylene glycol (EG), glycolic acid (GA), and ethanolamine (AA) at molar ratios of 1:5. This may be because these HBDs all contain hydroxyl groups which make them more likely to form hydrogen bonds with Pro (compared to the others). Therefore, these three DESs were used to extract methomyl from grain samples giving the results shown in Fig. 2 a It is clear that the Pro : EG DES yields higher extraction rates than those obtained with Pro : GA and Pro : AA (this may be because there are more hydroxyl groups in EG compared to the other two donors). Therefore, the Pro : EG DES was chosen to use in the subsequent experiments.

Changing the HBA to HBD molar ratio used to prepare the DES also has an effect on its extraction capacity [26]. The DES produced using a molar ratio of 1:1 readily solidified at room temperature, rendering it unsuitable for use as an extraction solvent in the current context. Therefore, DESs were prepared using Pro/EG molar ratios of 1:2, 1:3, 1:4, 1:5, 1:6, and 1:7 and then used to extract methomyl. The results are shown in Fig. 2b.

The highest extraction rate was obtained using a molar ratio of 1:5. Prior to this (range 1:2 to 1:5), the fluidity of the liquid improves (viscosity decreases) as the mole ratio of Pro decreases. This increase in fluidity leads to an increase in the extraction rate. Further decrease in the mole ratio of Pro (i.e. molar ratios from 1:5 to 1:7), however, leads to a gradual decrease in extraction efficiency. Therefore, a Pro/EG molar ratio of 1:5 was chosen for subsequent experiments.

If the volume of extraction solvent used is too small, then the analytes present cannot be extracted completely. On the other hand, too large a volume of extraction solvent reduces the sensitivity of the method leading to loss of analyte. Experiments were therefore conducted using a range of DES volumes (100, 150, 200, 250, 300, and 400 µL). The extraction results are shown in Fig. 2c. Clearly, the extraction rate gradually increases, peaks, and then decreases again as the volume of DES used increases. Therefore, 150 µL of DES is chosen to use in subsequent experiments.

Adding water to the DES can effectively reduce its viscosity and increase its hydrophilicity. However, the optimal water content also depends on the physical and chemical properties of the specific compounds involved [27,28]. Therefore, experiments were performed using different water contents (0%, 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, and 100%). Fig. S1 shows that the extraction rate decreased as the water content of the DES was gradually increased. The addition of water may break the hydrogen bonding that occurs between the HBA and HBD resulting in disruption to the extraction effect. Therefore, the DES was used without added water in subsequent experiments.

Extraction time is another parameter that has a significant effect on the extraction of target analytes (in general, more analyte is extracted as extraction time is increased). Vortex times of 30, 60, 90, 120, and 150 s were therefore used in a series of extraction experiments, giving the results shown in Fig. 2d.

As can be seen, the extraction rate gradually increased as the vortex time was increased up to about the 90-second mark. The extraction rate did not significantly increase beyond that point. It thus seems that 90 s is sufficient to fully extract the methomyl from the grains and so, 90 s could be chosen to use in subsequent extraction experiments.

Two experiments were performed to verify the feasibility of the proposed fluorescence detection method (Fig. 3 ). If methomyl is not added, the AChE catalyzes the hydrolysis of ATC, generating positively-charged TC with a sulfhydryl group. They interact with negatively-charged biomass CQDs and the sulfhydryl groups on TC cover the surface of biomass CQDs, which reduce the fluorescence intensity of biomass CQDs. If methomyl is added, however, methomyl preempts the binding with the serine active site of AChE, destroying its hydrolysis function of ATC to generate TC, so biomass CQDs fluorescence emission is strong [18]. The results shown in Fig. 3 therefore show that the proposed methomyl detection method is indeed feasible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of biomass used to generate CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Biomass CQDs are non-toxic and harmless. Millet, corn, wheat, and rice were selected to use as carbon sources to synthesize biomass CQDs in this work. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the strongest fluorescence was produced by the biomass CQDs synthesized from millet as a carbon source. Therefore, the biomass CQDs were selected to use as fluorescent probes in subsequent experiments. Further details of the potential characterization of the biomass CQDs synthesized are given in the Supplementary Materials.

Increasing the ATC concentration leads to the creation of more TC, which results in greater quenching of the fluorescence emitted from the biomass CQDs. Experiments were performed using different ATC concentrations (0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 1, 2, and 5 mmoL L–1), giving the results shown in Fig. 4b.

The intensity of the fluorescence from the CQDs gradually decreased at first as the ATC concentration was increased, and then it increased. The weakest fluorescence was produced when the ATC concentration was 1 mmoL L–1. Maybe because AChE catalyzed the hydrolysis of ATC to generate more TC when ATC concentration was 1 mmoL L–1, leading to stronger fluorescence quenching of biomass CQDs. So this concentration was selected for use in further experiments.

A higher concentration of AChE enables the ATC to hydrolyze more completely and hence produce more TC. Therefore, experiments were performed using different AChE concentrations (5, 10, 25, 50, 100, and 200 mU mL−1). The results are shown in Fig. 4c.

The fluorescence from the biomass CQDs gradually decreased in intensity at first as the AChE concentration was increased, and then it increased. When the AChE concentration was 50 mU mL−1, the intensity of the fluorescence was at its weakest. Probably because when the concentration of AChE was 50 mU mL−1, ATC generated more TC due to the catalytic hydrolysis, resulting in stronger fluorescence quenching of biomass CQDs. So this concentration of AChE was selected for use in subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the pH of the phosphate buffer
ce:section.ce:para: As AChE is a biological enzyme, its activity is highly dependent on pH, it may even be denatured under extreme pH conditions [29]. Therefore, the activity of AChE is closely related to the pH of the buffer. A series of experiments were performed using different pH values (6.2, 6.6, 6.8, 7.0, 7.4, and 7.8), giving the results shown in Fig. 4d.

The intensity of the fluorescence gradually decreased at first and then increased as the value of the pH was increased. The fluorescence was at its weakest when the pH was 6.8. A large amount of TC is generated at this pH thus quenching the fluorescence from the biomass CQDs. Therefore, pH 6.8 was selected for use in subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the interaction time between the TC and biomass CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The AChE catalyzes the hydrolysis of ATC to produce TC which subsequently interacts with the biomass CQDs to quench the emission of fluorescence. Therefore, a series of interaction times were used (5, 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 min), giving the results shown in Fig. 4e.

As the incubation time was increased, the intensity of the fluorescence from the biomass CQDs decreased rapidly at first and then leveled off at the 15 min mark. This indicates that the TC and biomass CQDs were fully interacted with each other within 15 min. Therefore, 15 min was selected as the optimized interaction time to use between the TC and biomass CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of incubation time between the methomyl solution and AChE
ce:section.ce:para: The incubation time between the methomyl and AChE was studied to ensure that the methomyl was given sufficient time to fully interact with the AChE. An increase in incubation time increases the inhibition of the AChE so that less TC is generated, resulting in stronger fluorescence emission from the biomass CQDs. Therefore, various incubation times were used (10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, and 70 min), giving the results shown in Fig. 4f.

The fluorescence intensity gradually increased at first and then decreased as the incubation time was increased. When the incubation time was 30 min, the fluorescence from the quantum dots reached a maximum. This indicates that methomyl extract solution sufficiently inhibited the activity of AChE when the incubation time was 30 min. Therefore, 30 min of incubation time was selected to use in subsequent reactions as this provides a sufficient amount of interaction between the extracted methomyl and AChE.

Solutions with different methomyl concentrations were used to calibrate the analysis technique (using the optimal extraction and detection conditions determined above). As the methomyl level was increased, the intensity of the fluorescence from the biomass CQDs gradually increased (Fig. S3).

The data shown in Fig. S3 suggest that a linear relationship exists between the fluorescence intensity and methomyl concentration over the concentration range from 0.01 to 5 mg kg−1. The data were subsequently treated using linear regression which yielded a calibration curve of the form
y =
5.8668 x + 29.7291
×
10 6
R 2
= 0.9926
.

The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated according to the formulae 3σ/K and 10σ/K, respectively, where σ is the relative standard deviation of the blank samples (based on 11 measurements) and K is the slope of the calibration curve. The LOD and LOQ were thus calculated to be 0.003 and 0.01 mg kg−1, respectively. Thus, the proposed method is a viable technique for extracting and detecting methomyl.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of methomyl in different grains
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method was applied to detecting methomyl in real samples. Blank samples of millet, rice, wheat, and barley were spiked with different concentrations of methomyl. Table 1 show the recovery subsequently obtained.

The table shows that the recovery ranged from 86.5% to 107.8% and that the relative standard deviations were all less than 3.8% (n = 3). Therefore, this method can be well applied to the extraction and detection of methomyl in real samples.

In this study, a new method for determining the concentration of methomyl in grain is established. The method uses a green DES to extract the methomyl which is subsequently assayed using a method of enzyme-inhibited fluorescence enhancement. The best DES to use was found to be that synthesized from L-proline and ethylene glycol. Biomass CQDs synthesized from millet were used as fluorescent probes to detect the presence of methomyl.

In the extraction process, the DES, the water content of DES, and the extraction time were optimized. In the detection process, the biomass CQDs, the ATC concentration, the AChE concentration, the pH of the phosphate buffer, the interaction time between the TC and biomass CQDs, and the incubation time between the methomyl solution and AChE were optimized.

Under optimal extraction and detection conditions, good linearity was found between the fluorescence response and methomyl concentration in the range 0.01–5 mg kg−1, The limit of detection was found to be 0.003 mg kg−1, and the limit of quantification 0.01 mg kg−1. Moreover, the extraction and detection system were successfully applied to analyze methomyl residues in millet, rice, wheat, and barley with satisfactory recovery and precision.
The method is environmentally friendly, highly efficient, and convenient to use. It thus has good application prospects in extracting and detecting pesticides that are present in food samples.

Yan Guo: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Huihui Wang: Conceptualization. Zhenjia Chen: Resources, Data curation. Xu Jing: Validation, Writing – original draft. Xiaowen Wang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by Shanxi Province Key R&D Plan [grant number 201903D211006]; Shanxi Agricultural University Science and Technology Innovation Fund [grant number 2017YJ35].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120412.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
